summary: Gruber recommends Craig Hockenberry's new book 'Making Sense of Color Management' from A Book Apart, framing it with a personal history of his own fascination with color displays on the Mac. He recounts choosing a Mac LC over the faster SE/30 in 1991 because it had a color display, and discovering how clever palette management could make 256 colors look far better than expected. He traces his connection to Hockenberry through The Iconfactory, then endorses the book as an excellent, concise guide that explains not just what to do about color management but why. The book addresses real-world problems like mismatched colors between CSS and graphics across browsers.
key_points: Gruber chose the slower Mac LC over the SE/30 in 1991 solely because it offered a color display, illustrating how compelling color was even at 256 colors
Early Mac color management required clever tricks like custom color palettes (CLUTs) to overcome hardware limitations, foreshadowing today's color management complexity
Hockenberry's book explains the why behind color management, not just procedural steps, applying Feynman's teaching principle as a measure of true understanding
Modern wide color gamut displays have made color management more complex, not simpler, making foundational knowledge essential for designers and developers
Apple's longstanding preference for humane descriptions over technical ones (e.g., 'Thousands' of colors instead of 65,536) reflects a design philosophy Gruber deeply admires
money_quotes: A color display was, for me, an irresistible draw.
I also remember thinking that 256 colors no longer seemed like 'a lot'.
The Iconfactory's developer was a very tall fellow named Craig Hockenberry.

You specify a certain exact RGB color in your CSS for a web page. Then you make a graphic for that web page, with the exact same RGB value for the background color. But when you put the graphic on the web page, the background colors don't match up. But only in some browsers, on some platforms. What the hell is going on?
In this book, Craig tells us not just what to do, but why. It's not merely a checklist of steps to follow blindly, but rather a foundation of knowledge.
This book is proof that Craig now truly understands how modern color management works.
It's only 91 pages long. It contains everything you need to know, and nothing you don't.
Color graphics have never been easy. As our technical capabilities have expanded, so has the complexity involved in understanding how it all works.
key_insight: Color management has grown more complex as display technology has advanced, making foundational understanding — not just procedural checklists — essential for anyone working with graphics.
